sectionName: Psychological, Social & Biological Foundations of Behavior

questions.passage: A wellness program compared three exercise-promotion messages: cash incentives, social accountability groups, and intrinsic-motivation coaching. Four-week attendance rates were 68%, 61%, and 54%, respectively. Eight weeks after incentives ended, attendance was 35%, 49%, and 52%. Participants in the intrinsic-coaching group reported the greatest enjoyment, while cash-incentive participants reported the strongest short-term external motivation.
questions.stem: According to the figure and passage, which pattern was observed?

choices.D: Cash incentives produced the highest short-term attendance but lower follow-up attendance after incentives ended
choices.B: The groups or conditions were identical
choices.C: The passage did not report any outcome differences

explanations.D: Correct. The passage identifies this specific relationship: Cash incentives produced the highest short-term attendance but lower follow-up attendance after incentives ended. This is the central empirical pattern around which the study or scenario is organized, and it is the pattern the later interpretation depends on.
explanations.A: The reported evidence does not support the reverse pattern.
explanations.B: The passage describes meaningful differences rather than identical outcomes.
explanations.C: The passage gives concrete outcomes or interview findings, so differences were reported.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 1. Exercise attendance during the intervention and after incentives ended.

questions.stem: Which concept best explains the scenario?

explanations.A: Correct. The scenario is best explained by intrinsic and extrinsic motivation because the key issue is that External rewards can boost behavior quickly but may not maintain it when rewards disappear; enjoyment and identity can support persistence. This connects the data to a core P/S framework rather than treating the finding as an isolated fact.
explanations.B: Classical conditioning is too narrow or unrelated to the social or cognitive process emphasized here.
explanations.C: Sensory adaptation concerns reduced responsiveness to constant sensory input, not the mechanism described.
explanations.D: The bystander effect concerns failures to help in groups and is not the main process in this passage.

questions.stem: What mechanism most plausibly accounts for the pattern?

choices.C: External rewards can boost behavior quickly but may not maintain it when rewards disappear; enjoyment and identity can support persistence.
choices.A: A random change in DNA sequence during the study
choices.B: A complete absence of social context
choices.D: The physical impossibility of changing behavior

explanations.C: Correct. The passage points to this mechanism directly: External rewards can boost behavior quickly but may not maintain it when rewards disappear; enjoyment and identity can support persistence. It explains why the observed groups or conditions differ and gives students a way to connect the empirical details to MCAT-level behavioral science.
explanations.A: The passage does not describe mutation or biological inheritance as the explanation.
explanations.B: The scenario is explicitly embedded in social, clinical, or developmental context.
explanations.D: The passage shows behavior or experience varying across conditions, so change is possible.

questions.stem: Which application would best follow from the passage?

choices.B: Combine initial structure with strategies that build enjoyment and self-determined reasons for exercise.
choices.A: Ignore the context and focus only on individual blame
choices.C: Use the least specific intervention possible
choices.D: Assume the finding applies identically to every person without adaptation

explanations.B: Correct. The best application is to Combine initial structure with strategies that build enjoyment and self-determined reasons for exercise. This response targets the mechanism described in the passage rather than relying on blame, vague advice, or one-size-fits-all reasoning.
explanations.A: Individual blame misses the contextual mechanism described in the passage.
explanations.C: A nonspecific intervention would not address the particular barrier or process identified.
explanations.D: P/S findings often guide interventions, but they still need adaptation to context and individual differences.

questions.stem: What is the most important limitation to keep in mind?

choices.A: The follow-up pattern suggests maintenance differences but does not prove one motivational pathway explains every participant.
choices.B: The passage cannot be interpreted because it contains words rather than equations
choices.C: Any behavioral study is automatically causal
choices.D: The topic is outside the Psychological/Social Foundations section

explanations.A: Correct. The key limitation is that The follow-up pattern suggests maintenance differences but does not prove one motivational pathway explains every participant. This keeps the inference aligned with the design and prevents overclaiming beyond what the passage can support.
explanations.B: P/S passages often use verbal descriptions and interviews; that does not make them uninterpretable.
explanations.C: Behavioral studies vary in design, and causal inference depends on assignment, controls, and measurement.
explanations.D: The concepts tested here are directly relevant to the P/S section.

questions.passage: A health psychology study interviewed students who reported stress-related snacking. Many described eating when anxious even when they were not physically hungry. Students who practiced emotion labeling and alternative coping plans reported fewer episodes of eating in response to stress during the following two weeks. The researchers distinguished physiological hunger from emotion-driven cues and emphasized that behavior change required recognizing the trigger before choosing a response.
questions.stem: According to the passage, which pattern was observed?

choices.D: Students often ate in response to anxiety rather than physiological hunger
choices.B: The groups or conditions were identical
choices.C: The passage did not report any outcome differences

explanations.D: Correct. The passage identifies this specific relationship: Students often ate in response to anxiety rather than physiological hunger. This is the central empirical pattern around which the study or scenario is organized, and it is the pattern the later interpretation depends on.
explanations.A: The reported evidence does not support the reverse pattern.
explanations.B: The passage describes meaningful differences rather than identical outcomes.
explanations.C: The passage gives concrete outcomes or interview findings, so differences were reported.

questions.stem: Which concept best explains the scenario?

explanations.C: Correct. The scenario is best explained by emotion and motivation because the key issue is that Stress can create learned or emotion-driven urges that feel compelling even without a homeostatic energy need. This connects the data to a core P/S framework rather than treating the finding as an isolated fact.
explanations.A: Classical conditioning is too narrow or unrelated to the social or cognitive process emphasized here.
explanations.B: Sensory adaptation concerns reduced responsiveness to constant sensory input, not the mechanism described.
explanations.D: The bystander effect concerns failures to help in groups and is not the main process in this passage.

questions.stem: What mechanism most plausibly accounts for the pattern?

choices.B: Stress can create learned or emotion-driven urges that feel compelling even without a homeostatic energy need.
choices.A: A random change in DNA sequence during the study
choices.C: A complete absence of social context
choices.D: The physical impossibility of changing behavior

explanations.B: Correct. The passage points to this mechanism directly: Stress can create learned or emotion-driven urges that feel compelling even without a homeostatic energy need. It explains why the observed groups or conditions differ and gives students a way to connect the empirical details to MCAT-level behavioral science.
explanations.A: The passage does not describe mutation or biological inheritance as the explanation.
explanations.C: The scenario is explicitly embedded in social, clinical, or developmental context.
explanations.D: The passage shows behavior or experience varying across conditions, so change is possible.

questions.stem: Which application would best follow from the passage?

choices.D: Teach emotion labeling and coping alternatives before focusing only on calories.
choices.A: Ignore the context and focus only on individual blame
choices.B: Use the least specific intervention possible
choices.C: Assume the finding applies identically to every person without adaptation

explanations.D: Correct. The best application is to Teach emotion labeling and coping alternatives before focusing only on calories. This response targets the mechanism described in the passage rather than relying on blame, vague advice, or one-size-fits-all reasoning.
explanations.A: Individual blame misses the contextual mechanism described in the passage.
explanations.B: A nonspecific intervention would not address the particular barrier or process identified.
explanations.C: P/S findings often guide interventions, but they still need adaptation to context and individual differences.

questions.stem: What is the most important limitation to keep in mind?

choices.A: Self-reported snacking can be biased, and the study does not prove labeling alone caused all improvements.
choices.B: The passage cannot be interpreted because it contains words rather than equations
choices.C: Any behavioral study is automatically causal
choices.D: The topic is outside the Psychological/Social Foundations section

explanations.A: Correct. The key limitation is that Self-reported snacking can be biased, and the study does not prove labeling alone caused all improvements. This keeps the inference aligned with the design and prevents overclaiming beyond what the passage can support.
explanations.B: P/S passages often use verbal descriptions and interviews; that does not make them uninterpretable.
explanations.C: Behavioral studies vary in design, and causal inference depends on assignment, controls, and measurement.
explanations.D: The concepts tested here are directly relevant to the P/S section.
